cards.definition: the framework of bones that supports the body, protects organs, and provides attachment points for muscles
smooth connective tissue covering the ends of bones at joints — reduces friction and absorbs shock
a tough band of connective tissue that joins bone to bone at a joint, providing stability
a tough band of connective tissue that joins muscle to bone, transmitting the force of muscle contractions
a freely movable joint lubricated by synovial fluid — e.g. knee (hinge), shoulder (ball and socket)
a synovial joint that allows movement in one plane only — e.g. knee, elbow, ankle
a synovial joint allowing movement in all directions — e.g. shoulder, hip
a movement that decreases the angle at a joint — e.g. bending the arm at the elbow
a movement that increases the angle at a joint — e.g. straightening the arm at the elbow
pairs of muscles that work against each other — one contracts (agonist) while the other relaxes (antagonist)
the number of times the heart beats per minute (bpm) — resting heart rate is typically 60–80 bpm
the volume of blood pumped out of the heart with each beat
the total volume of blood pumped by the heart per minute (Cardiac Output = Heart Rate × Stroke Volume)
energy production using oxygen — produces more ATP than anaerobic, used in sustained exercise
energy production without oxygen — produces lactic acid, used in short bursts of intense exercise
a waste product of anaerobic respiration that causes muscle fatigue and the burning sensation during intense exercise
the volume of air breathed in or out in a single breath
the number of breaths taken per minute — increases during exercise to supply more oxygen
the process of oxygen moving from the lungs into the blood, and carbon dioxide moving from the blood into the lungs
the maximum amount of oxygen a person can use during intense exercise — a key measure of aerobic fitness
Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type — the four variables that can be adjusted in a training programme
gradually increasing the demands of training so the body continues to adapt and improve
training should be relevant to the sport or skill being developed
fitness gains are lost if training stops — 'use it or lose it'
time for the body to repair and adapt after training — essential to prevent injury and overtraining
continuous, moderate-intensity exercise that improves cardiovascular endurance — e.g. long-distance running
alternating periods of high-intensity exercise with rest or low-intensity activity
a series of exercises performed at different stations, targeting different muscle groups
resistance exercises designed to increase muscle strength and size — e.g. weight training
stretching exercises designed to increase the range of movement at joints — e.g. yoga, static stretching
the drive to achieve a goal — intrinsic motivation comes from within; extrinsic motivation comes from external rewards
a state of mental and physical alertness — optimal arousal improves performance; too much or too little hinders it
feelings of worry and nervousness that can negatively affect performance — cognitive (mental) or somatic (physical)
the theory that performance improves as arousal increases up to an optimal point, then declines if arousal becomes too high
setting targets to focus effort and improve performance — SMART goals are Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Realistic, Time-bound
mentally practising a skill or performance before doing it — helps build confidence and improve technique
belief in one's ability to perform well — linked to better performance outcomes
hostile behaviour intended to harm — channelled aggression can aid performance but direct aggression is unacceptable
the degree to which team members work together effectively — social and task cohesion
guiding and influencing team members — autocratic (directive) vs democratic (collaborative) leadership styles
taking part in physical activity — barriers include cost, access, time, discrimination, and lack of role models
factors such as family, peers, culture, media, and role models that affect whether people take part in sport
unfair treatment based on characteristics such as gender, race, disability, or age — remains a significant issue in sport
the relationship between sport and television, social media, sponsorship — media increases participation and funding but can create negative pressures
the influence of money and business on sport — through sponsorship, TV rights, and merchandise
substances taken to improve sports performance — banned by sporting bodies for health and fairness reasons
fair, respectful, and ethical behaviour in sport — playing by the rules and respecting opponents
bending the rules or using psychological tactics to gain an advantage — legal but often seen as unsporting
a state of complete physical, mental, and social wellbeing — not just the absence of illness (WHO definition)
the ability to meet the demands of the environment — being fit enough to perform daily tasks and sport
